# Anypoint MQ Connector - Full Documentation

# Anypoint MQ ACK and NACK Operations

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Acknowledging a message (ACK) informs the Anypoint MQ broker that the message has been processed and
must be removed from the queue to prevent redelivery. Not acknowledging a message (NACK) tells the
broker to not process the message and to return the message to the queue for redelivery to any
available consumer.

## Acknowledgment Modes

The Anypoint MQ connector provides three acknowledgment modes:

- Immediate

  Use Immediate when you want to acknowledge messages as your app consumes them, before processing
  them.

- Automatic

  Use Automatic to acknowledge a received message automatically only if the flow execution finishes
  successfully.

- Manual

  Use Manual if you want the app logic to control message acknowledgment.

### Immediate Acknowledgment

Use the `IMMEDIATE` acknowledgment mode to acknowledge messages as your app consumes them and before
processing them.

When Anypoint MQ acknowledges a message, it removes the message from the queue and doesnât redeliver
it if an error occurs during processing.

If the app restarts between being acknowledged and dispatched, the message might not be available
again because it was deleted from the queue. To prevent message deletion until after processing, use
the `AUTO` or `MANUAL` acknowledgment mode instead.

If an error occurs during message processing using `IMMEDIATE` mode, Anypoint MQ throws an
`ANYPOINT-MQ:ACKING` error. To manage errors without losing messages, use a dead letter queue.

### Automatic Acknowledgment

Use the `AUTO` acknowledgment mode to acknowledge a received message automatically only if the flow
execution finishes successfully.

If an error occurs during flow execution, the message is not acknowledged (NACK) and the message is
returned to the queue for redelivery.

### Manual Acknowledgment

Use the `MANUAL` acknowledgment mode to delegate all responsibility for message acknowledgment to
the app logic.

With this configuration, every message received has an `ackToken` value that identifies this message
uniquely for a given connection.

Save this `ackToken` value to use later as input to the ACK or NACK operations.

## Acknowledgment Timeout

When using either `AUTO` or `MANUAL` acknowledgment modes, you can use the `acknowledgementTimeout`
parameter to control how long the message remains in-flight waiting to be acknowledged, before
automatically returning to the queue. The `acknowledgementTimeout` timer begins when the message is
consumed.

If `acknowledgementTimeout` is not set, Anypoint MQ Connector uses two minutes. If you need a
different TTL, you must define it explicitly in the `acknowledgementTimeout` parameter of the
subscriber.

When using `acknowledgementTimeout`, consider expected app-time processing, including time for
unexpected delays, such as external systems delay and app back-pressure due to high load. For
example, if you expect the consumed message to be processed in 10 seconds, set
`acknowledgementTimeout` to a minimum of 15 seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT] When using the `prefetch` mode in the Subscriber source, Anypoint MQ attempts to
> honor the expiration time specified by `acknowledgementTimeout`. However, Anypoint MQ extends the
> expiration timeout if the time that the message remains in the buffer reaches 80% of the timeout
> value.
>
> For this reason, donât use `prefetch` mode if the `acknowledgementTimeout` must be honored.

### Acknowledgment Timeout â AUTO Acknowledgement Mode

After a subscriber consumes a message, a timer starts, indicating how long the message has been
in-flight for the broker waiting for acknowledgment. If the flow processing does not end with a
success or failure before the `acknowledgementTimeout` limit is reached, the message automatically
returns to the queue for redelivery.

### Acknowledgment Timeout â MANUAL Acknowledgement Mode

When consuming a message using `MANUAL` acknowledgment mode by either the Subscriber or Consume
operations, the app must execute either an ACK or NACK operation using the message `ackToken` before
the `acknowledgementTimeout` limit is reached. If neither operation executes with the given token
before the timeout, the message automatically returns to the queue for redelivery. Any attempt to
acknowledge the message after the timeout limit fails due to an expired token.

## ACK Operation â MANUAL Acknowledgement Mode

Acknowledging a message (ACK) informs the broker that the message has been processed and must be
removed from the queue to prevent redelivery.

When performing the ACK operation using Manual mode, you must use the acknowledgment token
(`ackToken`) to identify the message. It is available in the `ackToken` field of the message
attributes:

```xml
<flow name="consumerWithManualAck">
    <scheduler>
        <scheduling-strategy >
          <fixed-frequency />
        </scheduling-strategy>
    </scheduler>

    <!-- Consume a message -->
    <anypoint-mq:consume destination="${destination}"
         acknowledgementMode="MANUAL"
            config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config"/>

    <!-- Save the ackToken for future acknowledgment -->
    <set-variable variableName="currentAckToken"
       value="#[attributes.ackToken]"/>

    <!-- Do the required processing of the message -->
    <flow-ref name="process-message"/>

    <!-- Use the ackToken to ACK the message -->
    <anypoint-mq:ack ackToken="#[vars.currentAckToken]"
         config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config" />
</flow>
```

If a non-void operation is invoked during message processing, the payload and attributes of the Mule
message are modified. To perform an ACK operation after processing, you must save `ackToken` in a
variable.

To save attributes to use later, use the `target` and `targetValue` parameters to store the whole
message in a variable:

```xml
<flow name="consumerWithManualAck">
      <scheduler>
        <scheduling-strategy >
          <fixed-frequency />
        </scheduling-strategy>
      </scheduler>

    <!-- Consume a message -->
    <anypoint-mq:consume destination="${destination}"
        acknowledgementMode="MANUAL"
        config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config"
        target="mqMessage"
        targetValue="#[message]"/>


    <!--Do any message processing-->
    <jms:publish-consume destination="#[vars.mqMessage.attributes.targetDestination]"
        config-ref="JMS_Config">
        <jms:message>
            <jms:body>#[vars.mqMessage.payload]</jms:body>
        </jms:message>
    </jms:publish-consume>

    <!-- Use the ackToken to ACK the message -->
    <anypoint-mq:ack ackToken="#[vars.mqMessage.attributes.ackToken]"
        config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config" />
</flow>
```

## NACK Operation

Not acknowledging (NACK) a message informs the broker that the message was not processed
successfully and commands the broker to return the message to the queue for redelivery to any
available consumer.

The acknowledgment token `ackToken` is a unique identifier for the message that you must use when
executing the NACK operation. It is available in the `ackToken` field of the message attributes:

```xml
<flow name="consumerWithManualAck">
    <scheduler>
      <scheduling-strategy >
        <fixed-frequency />
      </scheduling-strategy>
    </scheduler>

    <!-- Consume a message -->
    <anypoint-mq:consume destination="${destination}"
           acknowledgementMode="MANUAL"
           config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config"
           target="mqMessage"
          targetValue="#[message]"/>

    <!--Do message processing -->
    <logger message="#[payload]"/>

    <!-- Use the ackToken to NACK the message -->
    <anypoint-mq:nack ackToken="#[vars.mqMessage.attributes.ackToken]"
    config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config" />
</flow>
```

## See Also

- [Anypoint MQ Consume Operation](anypoint-mq-consume)
- [Anypoint MQ Subscriber Source](anypoint-mq-listener)

# Anypoint MQ Connector Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Support Version: Select

Anypoint MQ Connector Version 4.x

Anypoint MQ is a multi-tenant, cloud messaging service that enables customers to perform advanced
asynchronous messaging among their applications.

## Configurations

---

### Config

#### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Name</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Specifies the name for this configuration. Connectors refer to the configuration by using this name.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Connection</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#config_connection">Connection</a></p></div></div></td><td><p>Specifies the connection types to provide to this configuration.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Expiration Policy</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#ExpirationPolicy">Expiration Policy</a></p></div></div></td><td><p>Configures the minimum amount of time that a dynamic configuration instance can remain idle before the runtime considers it eligible for purging. The platform purges expired instances as needed to free resources.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Connection Types

##### Connection

###### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>URL</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>The region URL for the queue from the <strong>Anypoint Platform &gt; MQ</strong> console</p></td><td><p>https://mq-us-east-1.anypoint.mulesoft.com/api/v1</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Client App ID</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Value from either:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>ID</strong> from <strong>Anypoint Platform</strong> &gt; <strong>Access Management</strong> &gt; <strong>Connected Apps</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Client App ID</strong> from <strong>Anypoint Platform &gt; MQ &gt; Client Apps</strong></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Client Secret</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Value from either:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Secret</strong> from <strong>Anypoint Platform</strong> &gt; <strong>Access Management</strong> &gt; <strong>Connected Apps</strong></p></li><li><p><strong>Client Secret</strong> from <strong>Anypoint Platform &gt; MQ &gt; Client Apps</strong></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>TLS Context</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Tls">Tls</a></p></div></div></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Host</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Destination host for proxy requests</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Port</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Destination port for proxy requests</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Username</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Username to authenticate against the proxy</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Password</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Password to authenticate against the proxy</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Send Buffer Size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Size of the buffer (in bytes) used when sending data, set on the socket itself</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Receive Buffer Size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Size of the buffer (in bytes) used when receiving data</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Client Timeout</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>SO_TIMEOUT value on sockets. Indicates the amount of time (in milliseconds) that the socket waits in a blocking operation before failing. A value of 0 (the default) indicates an indefinite wait.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Send TCP With No Delay</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>If set, transmitted data is not grouped but sent immediately. Defaults to true.</p></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Linger</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>SO_LINGER value, which determines how long (in milliseconds) the socket takes to close so that any remaining data is transmitted correctly</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Keep Alive</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>SO_KEEPALIVE behavior on open sockets, which automatically checks open socket connections that are unused for long periods, and closes them if the connection becomes unavailable. This is a property on the socket itself and is used by a server socket to control whether connections to the server are kept alive before they are recycled.</p></td><td><p>false</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Connection Timeout</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Number of milliseconds to wait until an outbound connection to a remote server is successfully created, before failing with a timeout. Defaults to 30 seconds.</p></td><td><p>30000</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#Reconnection">Reconnection</a></p></div></div></td><td><p>When the application is deployed, a connectivity test is performed on all connectors. If set to true, deployment fails if the deployment connectivity test to each connector fails.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Operations

- [Ack](#ack)
- [Consume](#consume)
- [Nack](#nack)
- [Publish](#publish)

### Associated Sources

- [Subscriber](#subscriber)

### Ack

`<anypoint-mq:ack>`

Executes an acknowledgment indicating that the message is consumed correctly and deletes the message
from In Flight status.

#### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Configuration</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Name of the configuration to use</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Ack Token</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Acknowledgment token used to perform manual ACK and NACK operations, in MANUAL mode only</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#reconnect">Reconnect</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#reconnect-forever">Reconnect Forever</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Retry strategy in case of connectivity errors</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### For Configurations

- [Config](#config)

#### Throws

- ANYPOINT-MQ:ACKING
- ANYPOINT-MQ:CONNECTIVITY
- ANYPOINT-MQ:DESTINATION_NOT_FOUND
- ANYPOINT-MQ:RETRY_EXHAUSTED

### Consume

`<anypoint-mq:consume>`

#### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Configuration</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Name of the configuration to use</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Queue</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Queue or Exchange name from which to fetch a message</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Acknowledgment Mode</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>IMMEDIATE</p></li><li><p>MANUAL</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Acknowledgment mode to use for the messages retrieved</p></td><td><p>IMMEDIATE</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Acknowledgment Timeout</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Duration that a message is held by a consumer waiting for an acknowledgment or not acknowledgment. After that duration elapses, the message is again available to any consumer.</p></td><td><p>0</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Acknowledgment Timeout Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Time unit to use in the acknowledgmentTimeout configuration</p></td><td><p>MILLISECONDS</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Polling Time</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time to wait for a message to be ready for consumption (0-20000).</p></div><div><ul><li><p>If this value is greater than 0 and no message arrives, the connector throws a <code>ANYPOINT-MQ:TIMEOUT</code> exception.</p></li><li><p>If this value is 0 and no message arrives, the connector returns <code>NULL</code> and doesnât throw the <code>ANYPOINT-MQ:TIMEOUT</code> exception.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>10000</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Polling Time Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Time unit to use in the pollingTime configuration</p></td><td><p>MILLISECONDS</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Output Mime Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>MIME type of the operation output</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Output Encoding</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Encoding of the operation output</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Streaming Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#repeatable-in-memory-stream">Repeatable In Memory Stream</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#repeatable-file-store-stream">Repeatable File Store Stream</a></p></li><li><p>non-repeatable-stream</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Use repeatable streams</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Target Variable</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Name of a variable to store the operationâs output</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Target Value</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Expression to evaluate against the operationâs output and store the expression outcome in the target variable</p></td><td><p>#[payload]</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#reconnect">Reconnect</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#reconnect-forever">Reconnect Forever</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Retry strategy in case of connectivity errors</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p>Type</p></td><td><p>Binary</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Attributes Type</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#AnypointMQMessageAttributes">Anypoint MQ Message Attributes</a></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### For Configurations

- [Config](#config)

#### Throws

- ANYPOINT-MQ:ACKING
- ANYPOINT-MQ:CONSUMING
- ANYPOINT-MQ:CONNECTIVITY
- ANYPOINT-MQ:TIMEOUT
- ANYPOINT-MQ:DESTINATION_NOT_FOUND
- ANYPOINT-MQ:RETRY_EXHAUSTED

### Nack

`<anypoint-mq:nack>`

Executes a not acknowledgment and changes the status of the message from In Flight to In Queue for
subscriber consumption

#### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Configuration</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Name of the configuration to use</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Ack Token</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Acknowledgment token used to perform manual ACK and NACK operations, in MANUAL mode only</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#reconnect">Reconnect</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#reconnect-forever">Reconnect Forever</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Retry strategy in case of connectivity errors</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### For Configurations

- [Config](#config)

#### Throws

- ANYPOINT-MQ:NACKING
- ANYPOINT-MQ:CONNECTIVITY
- ANYPOINT-MQ:DESTINATION_NOT_FOUND
- ANYPOINT-MQ:RETRY_EXHAUSTED

### Publish

`<anypoint-mq:publish>`

#### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Configuration</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Name of the configuration to use</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Destination</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Queue or Exchange name from which to fetch a message</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Output Mime Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>MIME type of the operation output</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Output Encoding</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>The encoding of the operation output</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Streaming Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#repeatable-in-memory-stream">Repeatable In Memory Stream</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#repeatable-file-store-stream">Repeatable File Store Stream</a></p></li><li><p>non-repeatable-stream</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Use repeatable streams</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Body</p></td><td><div><div><p>Binary</p></div></div></td><td><p>Body of the outgoing message, sent as a string as part of a JSON message. Any content that is published must be capable of being serialized to and deserialized from string format. Sending Objects that donât have a proper string serialization causes the message to be unreadable on the receiving end.</p></td><td><p>#[payload]</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>User Properties</p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><p>Custom user properties set to the message</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message Id</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Valid custom user ID for the outgoing message. If not specified, the broker creates one.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message Group Id</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>ID of the message group to which the published message belongs. Specify only for FIFO queues.</p><p>Message group IDs can contain up to 128 alphanumeric characters (a-z, A-Z, 0-9) and punctuation (! " # $ % &amp; ' ( ) * + , - . / : ; &lt; = &gt; ? @ [ ] \ ^ _ ` { } | ~).</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Delivery Delay</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Time to postpone delivery of the message before consumption: between 0 and 15 minutes.</p></td><td><p>MILLISECONDS</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Delivery Delay Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time unit to use in the <code>Delivery Delay</code> parameter:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>MILLISECONDS</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Send Content-Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Set to true if the body type should be sent as an Anypoint MQ Message property</p></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Target Variable</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Name of a variable to store the operation output</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Target Value</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Expression to evaluate against the operation output. The expression outcome is stored in the target variable</p></td><td><p>#[payload]</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#reconnect">Reconnect</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#reconnect-forever">Reconnect Forever</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Retry strategy in case of connectivity errors</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Output

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p>Type</p></td><td><p>Binary</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Attributes Type</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#AnypointMQMessagePublishAttributes">Anypoint MQ Message Publish Attributes</a></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### For Configurations

- [Config](#config)

#### Throws

- ANYPOINT-MQ:PUBLISHING
- ANYPOINT-MQ:CONNECTIVITY
- ANYPOINT-MQ:ILLEGAL_BODY
- ANYPOINT-MQ:DESTINATION_NOT_FOUND
- ANYPOINT-MQ:RETRY_EXHAUSTED

## Sources

### Subscriber

`<anypoint-mq:subscriber>`

Anypoint MQ Subscriber Message Source retrieves messages from the given destination name.

#### Parameters

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Configuration</p></td><td><p>String</p></td><td><p>Name of the configuration to use</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Queue</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Name of the queue from which messages are retrieved</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Subscriber Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>One of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#polling">Polling</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#prefetch">Prefetch</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Strategy to use when subscribing to messages from the service</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Acknowledgment Mode</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>IMMEDIATE</p></li><li><p>AUTO</p></li><li><p>MANUAL</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Acknowledgment mode to use for the messages retrieved</p></td><td><p>AUTO</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Acknowledgment Timeout</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Duration that a message is held by a broker waiting for an acknowledgment or not acknowledgment before being made available to any subscriber. Specifies the default <code>acknowledgmentTimeout</code> value for all Subscriber sources, unless it is overridden. The maximum value of this parameter is 12 hours.</p></td><td><p>If <code>acknowledgementTimeout</code> is not set (the default), the connector uses the TTL value of at least two minutes.</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Acknowledgment Timeout Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Time unit to use in the acknowledgmentTimeout configuration</p></td><td><p>MILLISECONDS</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Circuit Breaker</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#circuitBreaker">Circuit Breaker</a></p></div></div></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Output Mime Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>MIME type of the operation output</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Output Encoding</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Encoding of the operation output</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Primary Node Only</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Whether this source should only be executed on the primary node when running in a cluster</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Streaming Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#repeatable-in-memory-stream">Repeatable In Memory Stream</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#repeatable-file-store-stream">Repeatable File Store Stream</a></p></li><li><p>non-repeatable-stream</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Use repeatable streams</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Redelivery Policy</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#RedeliveryPolicy">Redelivery Policy</a></p></div></div></td><td><p>Message redelivery policy</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#reconnect">Reconnect</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#reconnect-forever">Reconnect Forever</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Retry strategy in case of connectivity errors</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Output

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p>Type</p></td><td><p>Binary</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Attributes Type</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#AnypointMQMessageAttributes">Anypoint MQ Message Attributes</a></p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### For Configurations

- [Config](#config)

## Types

### Tls

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Enabled Protocols</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Comma-separated list of protocols enabled for this context</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Enabled Cipher Suites</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Comma-separated list of cipher suites enabled for this context</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Trust Store</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#TrustStore">Trust Store</a></p></div></div></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Key Store</p></td><td><div><div><p><a href="#KeyStore">Key Store</a></p></div></div></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Revocation Check</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#standard-revocation-check">Standard Revocation Check</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#custom-ocsp-responder">Custom OCSP Responder</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#crl-file">CRL File</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Trust Store

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Path</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Location of the trust store, resolved relative to the current classpath and file system, if possible</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Password</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Password used to protect the trust store</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Type of store used</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Algorithm</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Algorithm used by the trust store</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Insecure</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>If true, no certificate validations are performed, rendering connections vulnerable to attacks. Use at your own risk.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Key Store

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Path</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Location of the key store, resolved relative to the current classpath and file system, if possible</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Type of store used</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Alias</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>When the key store contains many private keys, indicates the alias of the key that is used. If not defined, the first key in the file is used by default.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Key Password</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Password used to protect the private key</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Password</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Password used to protect the key store</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Algorithm</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Algorithm used by the key store</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Standard Revocation Check

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Only End Entities</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Verifies only the last element in the certificate chain</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Prefer Crls</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Uses CRL-signing certificates before using OCSP-signing certificate</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>No Fallback</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Doesnât use the secondary checking method (the one not selected for Prefer Crls)</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Soft Fail</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>Avoids verification failure when the revocation server cannot be reached or is busy</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Custom OCSP Responder

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Url</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>URL of the OCSP responder</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Cert Alias</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Alias of the signing certificate for the OCSP response, if present. The signing certificate must be in the trust store.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### CRL File

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Path</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Path to the CRL file</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Reconnection

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Fails Deployment</p></td><td><div><div><p>Boolean</p></div></div></td><td><p>When the application is deployed, a connectivity test is performed on all connectors. If set to true, deployment fails if the deployment connectivity test to each connector fails.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Reconnection Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#reconnect">Reconnect</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#reconnect-forever">Reconnect Forever</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Reconnection strategy to use</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Reconnect

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Frequency</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>How often to reconnect, in milliseconds</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Count</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>How many reconnection attempts to make</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>blocking</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><p>If false, the reconnection strategy runs in a separate, non-blocking thread</p></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Reconnect Forever

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Frequency</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>How often to reconnect, in milliseconds</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>blocking</p></td><td><p>Boolean</p></td><td><p>If false, the reconnection strategy runs in a separate, non-blocking thread</p></td><td><p>true</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Expiration Policy

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Max Idle Time</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>A scalar time value for the maximum amount of time a dynamic configuration instance should be allowed to be idle before itâs considered eligible for expiration</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Time Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>A time unit that qualifies the maxIdleTime attribute</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Anypoint MQ Message Attributes

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Destination</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Message destination</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Headers</p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><p>Message headers</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Properties</p></td><td><div><div><p>Object</p></div></div></td><td><p>Message properties</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Message Id</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>ID of the message</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Delivery Count</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Number of times the message was delivered</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Content Type</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Content type of the message body</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Ack Token</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Acknowledgment token used to perform manual ACK and NACK operations, in MANUAL mode only</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Circuit Breaker

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>On Error Types</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>Error types that equal failure of the circuit when they occur during flow execution. By default, all errors result in failure.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Errors Threshold</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Of the errors considered by the circuit, how many must occur for the circuit to be opened.</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Trip Timeout</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>How long the circuit remains open after <code>errorsThreshold</code> is reached</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Trip Timeout Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>NANOSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MICROSECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>DAYS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>The time unit bound to the tripTimeout value</p></td><td><p>MILLISECONDS</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Repeatable In Memory Stream

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Initial Buffer Size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>The amount of memory allocated to consume the stream and provide random access to it. If the stream contains more data than can fit into this buffer, then the buffer expands according to the bufferSizeIncrement attribute, with an upper limit of maxInMemorySize.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Buffer Size Increment</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>How much buffer size can expand. If set to 0 (zero) or less, STREAM_MAXIMUM_SIZE_EXCEEDED is raised when the buffer is full.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Max Buffer Size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Maximum amount of memory to use before STREAM_MAXIMUM_SIZE_EXCEEDED is raised. If set to 0 (zero) or less, memory is unlimited.</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Buffer Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Unit in which all these attributes are expressed</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Repeatable File Store Stream

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Max In Memory Size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Maximum amount of memory that the stream can hold locally before content is buffered to disk</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Buffer Unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>BYTE</p></li><li><p>KB</p></li><li><p>MB</p></li><li><p>GB</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Unit in which maxInMemorySize is expressed</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Redelivery Policy

For information on the redelivery policy, see
[Configure a Redelivery Policy](../../mule-runtime/latest/redelivery-policy).

### Anypoint MQ Message Publish Attributes

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Message Id</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>ID of the message that was published</p></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Prefetch

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Max Local Messages</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Maximum number of messages to prefetch from the service and keep locally before dispatching to the flow.</p><p>NOTE: Due to a known issue, using <code>prefetch</code> mode with <code>maxConcurrency="1"</code> set on the <code>flow</code> element might result in message-processing latency.</p><p>When using <code>prefetch</code> mode, set <code>maxLocalMessages</code> to a value greater than <code>1</code> to avoid message-processing latency issues. When <code>maxLocalMessages</code> is set to <code>1</code>, latency can occur when the connector prefetches the message but the app doesnât consume it, and the message returns to the queue for reprocessing.</p></td><td><p>30</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Polling

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Fetch size</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>The maximum number of messages (1-10) to fetch on each polling execution</p><p>An error occurs if you configure a value greater than 10.</p></td><td><p>10</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Scheduling Strategy</p></td><td><div><div><p>scheduling-strategy</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Scheduling strategy for triggering the message fetch from the service</p></div><div><ul><li><p><a href="#fixed-frequency">Fixed Frequency</a></p></li><li><p><a href="#cron">Cron</a></p></li></ul></div></div></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Fixed Frequency

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Frequency</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>Polling frequency</p></td><td><div><div><p>1000ms</p></div><div><p>(1 second)</p></div></div></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Start delay</p></td><td><div><div><p>Number</p></div></div></td><td><p>The amount of time for the scheduler to wait before starting</p></td><td><p>10</p></td><td></td></tr><tr><td><p>Time unit</p></td><td><div><div><p>Enumeration, one of:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>DAYS</p></li><li><p>HOURS</p></li><li><p>MINUTES</p></li><li><p>SECONDS</p></li><li><p>MILLISECONDS</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Time unit to use in the <code>startDelay</code> configuration</p></td><td><p>MILLISECONDS</p></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Cron

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Type</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Expression</p></td><td><div><div><p>Cron expression</p></div></div></td><td></td><td></td><td><p>x</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Time Zone</p></td><td><div><div><p>String</p></div></div></td><td><p>The ID of the time zone in which the expression is based</p></td><td></td><td></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Fixed Frequency](../../mule-runtime/latest/scheduler-concept#fixed-frequency)
- [Cron Expressions](../../mule-runtime/latest/scheduler-concept#cron-expressions)
---

# Upgrading and Migrating Anypoint MQ Connector

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

To use the latest features, upgrade Anypoint Connector for Anypoint MQ (Anypoint MQ Connector) from
version 3.x to version 4.x.

## Supported Upgrade Paths

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>From Version</th><th>To Version</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>3.x</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>4.x</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Changes in This Release

Some changes included in version 4.x break backward compatibility with version 3.x.

Anypoint MQ Connector 4.x includes support for these features:

- Cross-Region Failover

  Anypoint MQ Connector for Mule 4 version 4.0.7 and later provides failover capability for standard
  queues.

  When enabled, if a Mule app canât reach an Anypoint MQ server in a region, the connector switches
  to the fallback queue in the fallback region to publish and consume messages.

  For information, see:
  - [Configuring Cross-Region Failover for Standard Queues](../../mq/mq-failover)
  - [Configuring Cross-Region Failover in Your App](anypoint-mq-failover)

- Connected Apps

  Anypoint MQ Connector for Mule 4 version 4.0.1 and later includes a new format for the **URL**
  field in the **Global Element Properties** page.
  - In Anypoint MQ Connector 3.x, the URL format was:

    `https://mq-us-east-1.anypoint.mulesoft.com/api/v1`

  - In Anypoint MQ Connector 4.x, the URL format includes `{orgID}` and `{envID}`. For example:

    `https://mq-us-east-1.anypoint.mulesoft.com/api/v1/organizations/{orgID}/environments/{envID}`

  > [!IMPORTANT] If you configure the connector with the wrong version of the URL, Anypoint MQ
  > returns the following error:
  >
  > `Invalid api url provided`

To use the failover feature, see
[Configuring Cross-Region Failover in Your App](anypoint-mq-failover).

## Upgrade Prerequisites

Before you perform the upgrade, create a backup of your files, data, and configurations in case you
need to revert to the previous version.

## Upgrade Steps

To upgrade to Anypoint MQ Connector 4.x:

1.  In Anypoint MQ, in the **Destinations** page, click **Copy** and select **Connector version 4.x
    and later** from the menu:

    ![Copy button and menu with connector version options](../../mq/_images/mq-tutorial-url.png)

2.  In Anypoint Studio, open the Mule project.
3.  In the Mule Palette view, click **Search in Exchange**.
4.  In **Add Dependencies to Project**, type `Anypoint MQ` in the search field.
5.  In **Available modules**, select **Anypoint MQ Connector - Mule 4** and then click **Add**.
6.  Click **Finish**.

    Anypoint Studio upgrades the connector automatically.

7.  Verify that the `mq-connector` dependency version is `4.0.5` or later in the `pom.xml` file:

    ```xml
    <dependency>
    	<groupId>com.mulesoft.connectors</groupId>
    	<artifactId>anypoint-mq-connector</artifactId>
    	<version>4.0.5</version>
    	<classifier>mule-plugin</classifier>
    </dependency>
    ```

8.  In Anypoint Studio, in the **Anypoint MQ Config** window, paste the URL in the **URL** field:

    ![Anypoint MQ Config page displaying the URL, client app ID, and client secret](../../mq/_images/mq-tutorial-studio7-mq-config.png)

9.  Depending on your authentication method, complete the **Client App ID** and **Client Secret**
    fields:
    - **Connected App**:

      Paste the values of **ID** and **Secret** from **Anypoint Platform** > **Access Management** >
      **Connected Apps**.

      For information about configuring Connected Apps in Anypoint MQ, see
      [Configuring Connected Apps](../../mq/mq-connected-apps).

    - **Client App**:

      Paste the values of **Client App ID** and **Client Secret** from **Anypoint Platform** >
      **MQ** > **Client Apps**.

      For information about configuring Client Apps in Anypoint MQ, see
      [Configuring Client Apps](../../mq/mq-client-apps).

## Post Upgrade Steps

After you install the latest version of the connector, follow these steps to complete the upgrade:

1.  In Anypoint Studio, verify that there are no errors in the **Problems** or **Console** views.
2.  Check the project pom.xml and verify that there are no problems.
3.  Test the connection and verify that the operations work.

## Troubleshooting

If you experience problems with caching the parameters and caching the metadata, restart Anypoint
Studio.

### Reverting the Upgrade

To revert to the previous version of Anypoint MQ Connector, in Anypoint Studio, change the
`mq-connector` dependency version in the projectâs `pom.xml` file to the previous version.

## See Also

- [Introduction to Anypoint Connectors](../../connectors/introduction/introduction-to-anypoint-connectors)
- [Salesforce Help](https://help.salesforce.com)
---

# Anypoint MQ Consume Operation

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The Consume operation in the Anypoint MQ connector enables the app to consume a single message from
a queue at any time in the flow using the acknowledgment strategy that supports your use case.

> [!NOTE] Anypoint MQ supports consuming from queues, not message exchanges.
>
> If an app attempts to consume from a message exchange, the Anypoint MQ REST API throws the
> following error: `The destination should be a queue.`

## Incoming Message Structure

Every incoming message consists of two parts:

- `payload` - Represents the message body
- `attributes` - Contains metadata for the received message, such as its ID or other properties

![Mule message, including payload and message attributes](_images/amq-4x-consume-attributes.png)

## Message Consumption

The simplest way to consume a message from a queue is to drag the **Consume** operation from the
Mule palette to the canvas and configure the `Queue` parameter with the name of the queue from which
to retrieve the message:

![Connector configuration, including destination, acknowledgment mode and timeout, and polling timeout](_images/amq-4x-consume-operation.png)

```xml
<anypoint-mq:consume config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_config" destination="myQueue"/>
```

The Consume operation in this example consumes the first available message in `myQueue` and converts
it to a `MuleMessage` instance with this structure:

- The message body as payload.
- The message metadata in the message attributes. Attributes might include `messageId` or
  `redeliveryCount`.

Use a DataWeave expression in the `Queue` parameter to dynamically change the target queue from
which to consume the message.

## Consumed Message Acknowledgment

- **Automatic**

  By default, `acknowledgementMode` is `IMMEDIATE`, meaning that the message is acknowledged as soon
  as it is consumed from the queue and before itâs delivered to the Mule flow. If the message
  acknowledgment fails, the Consume operation terminates with an `ANYPOINT-MQ:ACKING` error.

  For more information, see [Immediate Acknowledgment](anypoint-mq-ack#immediate-acknowledgment).

- **Manual**

  To manually control the acknowledgment of the message, change `acknowledgementMode` to `MANUAL`.
  When using the `MANUAL` acknowledgment mode, the app logic decides when to perform the
  acknowledgment of the message, using the [ACK](anypoint-mq-ack) or [NACK](anypoint-mq-ack)
  operations.

  To perform the manual acknowledgment, you need the value of `ackToken` provided as part of the
  resulting message attributes.

  For more information, see [Manual Acknowledgment](anypoint-mq-ack#manual-acknowledgment).

For information about acknowledgment timeouts, see
[Acknowledgment Timeout](anypoint-mq-ack#acknowledgment-timeout).

## Empty Queue Management

When a queue has messages, the Consume operation returns immediately with one of the messages.

When a queue is empty, the operation behavior depends on the value of the `pollingTime` parameter:

- No value

  By default, the Consume operation waits for 10000 milliseconds (10 seconds) for a new message to
  arrive when consuming from an empty queue.

  If Anypoint MQ receives no messages in that time, Consume fails with an `ANYPOINT-MQ:TIMEOUT`
  error.

- `0`

  If the value of `pollingTime` is `0`, the Consume operation waits for the default of 10000
  milliseconds (10 seconds).

  If Anypoint MQ receives no messages in that time, Consume produces a `null` message as the result
  and doesnât produce an error.

- `1` to `20000`

  If the value of `pollingTime` is between one and 20000 milliseconds (the maximum value), Consume
  waits for the time specified by `pollingTime` for a new message to arrive.

  If Anypoint MQ receives no messages within the given time window, Consume fails with an
  `ANYPOINT-MQ:TIMEOUT` error.

- If the value of `pollingTime` is outside the range of 0-20000 milliseconds (20 seconds), Consume
  fails with an `ANYPOINT-MQ:CONSUMING` error.

## Output Message Metadata

Each message received consists of two parts: `Payload` and `Attributes`. `Attributes` contains the
metadata for the message.

This metadata maps all the information available in an Anypoint MQ message, including:

- Message ID
- Headers Map
- Properties Map

![Mule message, including payload and message attributes](_images/amq-4x-consume-attributes.png)

For information about the `attributes` structure, see the Consume operation in the
[Anypoint MQ Connector Reference - 4.x](anypoint-mq-connector-reference#consume).

## See Also

- [Anypoint MQ ACK and NACK Operations](anypoint-mq-ack)
---

# Configuring Cross-Region Failover in Your App

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint MQ Connector for Mule 4 version 4.0.7 and later provides failover capability for standard
queues.

This feature is not enabled by default. Before using this feature, review
[Configuring Cross-Region Failover for Standard Queues](../../mq/mq-failover).

> [!IMPORTANT] When you enable failover for a queue and the primary region goes down, your data
> might reside in a region outside the country until the primary region is available

To use the failover feature, do the following:

- In your app:
  1.  Upgrade Anypoint MQ Connector to version 4.0.7 or later.
  2.  [Enable failover in your app.](#enable-failover-feature)
  3.  (Optional) [Change the check frequencies](#configure-check-frequency).

- In Anypoint MQ:
  1.  Create or modify a queue and
      [enable failover on the queue](../../mq/mq-failover#enable-failover).

## Enable Failover in Your App

To enable the failover feature in your app, set the `cross.region.failover.feature.enabled` system
property to `true`. This property is set to `false` by default.

> [!NOTE] When the failover property is enabled, the connector handles processing messages for both
> the primary and failover queues. To prevent interruptions, the app canât publish or consume
> directly from the fallback queue. See
> [Process Messages Directly from the Fallback Queue](#consume-directly-fallback) for information.

To set this application property in Runtime Manager:

1.  Sign in to Anypoint Platform and click **Runtime Manager** in the navigation menu.
2.  Deploy a new app or change the settings for a deployed app.
3.  On the **Properties** tab, specify the value for the property, for example:

    ![Properties table with cross.region.failover.feature.enabled set to true](_images/mq-set-properties-failover-enabled.png)

Alternatively, set the property in **Run Configurations** in Anypoint Studio. For more information,
see [System Properties in Mule Apps](../../mule-runtime/latest/mule-app-properties-system).

## Process Messages Directly from the Fallback Queue

If failover is enabled for an app, Anypoint MQ Connector manages the fallback queue (with the `_fb`
suffix) and prevents publish, consume, or subscribe operations on the fallback queue directly. If
the app connects to the fallback queue directly, Anypoint MQ Connector returns an error:

```none
java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Using fallback queue directly is not supported
```

If you disable failover on a queue (from the Anypoint MQ UI) and restart the application, the
connector no longer publishes or consumes from the fallback queue.

To process any messages that remain in the fallback queue after disabling failover:

1.  Configure the app to process directly from the fallback queue.

    For the Consume or Subscriber operations, set the **Queue** field to the name of the fallback
    queue, for example:

    ![Subscriber configuration with the queue name, myDemoQueue_fb](_images/mq-publish-fallback-queue-subscribe.png)

2.  Allow the app to consume messages directly from the fallback queue:
    1.  Sign in to Anypoint Platform and click **Runtime Manager** in the navigation menu.
    2.  Change the settings for a deployed app.
    3.  On the **Properties** tab, set the `cross.region.failover.feature.enabled` system property
        to `false`, for example:

        ![Properties table with cross.region.failover.feature.enabled set to false](_images/mq-set-properties-failover-disabled.png)

        Alternatively, set the property in **Run Configurations** in Anypoint Studio. For more
        information, see
        [System Properties in Mule Apps](../../mule-runtime/latest/mule-app-properties-system).

## Configure the Frequency of Fallback Queue Checks

By default, when the primary region is down, Anypoint MQ Connector checks the:

- Fallback queue once every five minutes for new messages (300,000 milliseconds)
- Primary queue to see if the primary region is back up once per minute (60,000 milliseconds)

To change this behavior, set the following properties:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Property</th><th>Description</th><th>Minimum Value</th><th>Maximum Value</th><th>Default Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td colspan="5"><p><code>primary.region.status.check.interval.ms</code></p></td></tr><tr><td></td><td><p>How often the connector checks the availability of the primary region when processing from the fallback region</p></td><td><p>1 minute (60,000 milliseconds)</p></td><td><p>15 minutes (900,000 milliseconds)</p></td><td><p>1 minute (60,000 milliseconds)</p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="5"><p><code>fallback.messages.check.interval.ms</code></p></td></tr><tr><td></td><td><p>How often the connector checks the fallback queue for received messages</p></td><td><p>1 minute (60,000 milliseconds)</p></td><td><p>12 hours (43.2 million milliseconds)</p></td><td><p>5 minutes (300,000 milliseconds)</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

To set these application properties in Runtime Manager:

1.  Sign in to Anypoint Platform and click **Runtime Manager** in the navigation menu.
2.  Deploy a new app or change the settings for a deployed app.
3.  On the **Properties** tab, specify values for the following properties (in milliseconds):

    ![Properties table with intervals for primary and fallback status check intervals](_images/mq-set-properties-failover.png)

Alternatively, set these properties in **Run Configurations** in Anypoint Studio. For more
information, see
[System Properties in Mule Apps](../../mule-runtime/latest/mule-app-properties-system)

### Check Interval Considerations

To determine how to set the application property values to fine-tune the check intervals:

- When publishing to the fallback region during primary region downtime, the Publish operation of
  Anypoint MQ Connector checks if the primary region is back up once per minute by default.

  If a flow triggers the Publish operation less frequently than once per minute, the connector
  checks to see if the primary region is back up every time the operation is triggered.

  To fine-tune this behavior, set the value of `primary.region.status.check.interval.ms` to be
  greater than the frequency at which your flow triggers the Publish operation.

- When a flow triggers the Consumer or Subscriber operation of Anypoint MQ Connector, the connector
  checks the fallback queue (every five minutes by default) for new messages before checking the
  primary queue.

  If a flow triggers the Consumer or Subscriber operation less frequently than every five minutes,
  the connector first checks the fallback queue for new messages before consuming from the primary
  queue.

  To reduce the frequency of checking the fallback queue, set the value of
  `fallback.messages.check.interval.ms` to be greater than the frequency at which your flow triggers
  the Consumer or Subscriber operation.

  > [!NOTE] Setting this property to a high value might result in out-of-order messages because
  > messages in the fallback queue are not processed as frequently.

## See Also

- [Configuring Cross-Region Failover for Standard Queues](../../mq/mq-failover)
- [Changing App Behavior with Properties on CloudHub 2.0](../../cloudhub-2/ch2-manage-props)
- [Manage Properties for Applications on CloudHub](../../cloudhub/cloudhub-manage-props)
- [Anypoint MQ Connector in Anypoint Exchange](https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/exchange/com.mulesoft.connectors/anypoint-mq-connector/)
- [Anypoint MQ Connector Release Notes for Mule 4](../../release-notes/connector/anypoint-mq-connector-release-notes-mule-4)
---

# Anypoint MQ Subscriber Source

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The Subscriber source in the Anypoint MQ connector enables the app to listen for new messages and
consume them as they arrive at the destination. You can configure different listening strategies
that enable you to tune the consumption for performance, predictability, and schedules.

## Maximum Message Throughput

By default, the Subscriber source works in continuous listening mode, providing maximum throughput
by consuming messages as soon as they arrive in the queue. This is called the `prefetch`
subscriber-type configuration. When using the `prefetch` mode, the Subscriber source attempts to
keep a local buffer of messages full, making them available to be dispatched to the Mule flow as
soon as the app can accept them.

![Subscriber configuration window showing Subscriber Type set to Prefetch](_images/amq-4x-subscriber-prefetch.png)

> [!NOTE] Due to a known issue, using `prefetch` mode with `maxConcurrency="1"` set on the `flow`
> element might result in message-processing latency.

For the `prefetch` subscriber-type configuration, you configure the `maxLocalMessages` parameter to
specify the target size for a full buffer, which controls how quickly messages are taken from the
queue.

```xml
<anypoint-mq:subscriber config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config" destination="myQueue">
</anypoint-mq:subscriber>
```

- Use a larger buffer size for maximum performance, keeping in mind that the messages kept locally
  wonât be available for any other consumer.

  When a consumer takes a message from the queue and stores it in the local buffer, the message
  appears as in-flight for any other consumer and prevents them from consuming it. The message
  remains in this in-flight state for as long as necessary for the app to begin the processing.

- Use a smaller buffer size to process messages as they are published to the queue.

  The smaller buffer restricts app-level throughput and avoids blocking competing consumers.

  When using `prefetch` mode, set `maxLocalMessages` to a value greater than `1` to avoid
  message-processing latency issues. When `maxLocalMessages` is set to `1`, latency can occur when
  the connector prefetches the message but the app doesnât consume it, and the message returns to
  the queue for reprocessing.

### FIFO Queues and Prefetch Mode

When using the `prefetch` mode, the `acknowledgementTimeout` timer begins when the message is taken
from the queue. Anypoint MQ attempts to honor the expiration time specified by
`acknowledgementTimeout`. However, Anypoint MQ extends the expiration timeout if the time that the
message remains in the buffer reaches 80% of the timeout value.

Donât use `prefetch` mode in the following situations:

- Applications with long processing times that use the `maxConcurrency` attribute to limit
  concurrent processing.

  These options can cause messages to remain in the buffer longer, which can result in the messages
  expiring and being sent back to the queue.

- When using FIFO queues

  Because Anypoint MQ canât guarantee ordering when using `prefetch` mode, donât use it with FIFO
  queues.

  Using `prefetch` mode for applications with long processing times can consume significant
  resources because Anypoint MQ monitors the ACK TTL for all messages in the local buffer and
  extends the TTL to avoid sending messages back to the queue.

## Predictable Message Consumption

If your app requires predictable and controlled message consumption rather than maximum throughput,
you can configure the Subscriber as a polling source. The Subscriber source checks for new messages
in the queue at a fixed scheduling rate. Every time the poll is triggered, the Subscriber source
retrieves between 1 and 10 messages to dispatch to the flow individually in order.

Messages arenât consumed immediately. Instead, they remain in the queue until the next poll of the
Subscriber source is triggered. The queue contains up to 10 messages to be retrieved. For example,
if your queue has 15 messages, one poll consumes the first 10 messages and the remaining 5 messages
wait for the next poll.

To configure the Subscriber as a polling source, specify the `polling` subscriber-type.

The default `polling` subscriber-type declaration looks like this:

![Subscriber configuration window showing Subscriber Type set to Polling](_images/amq-4x-subscriber-polling.png)

```xml
<anypoint-mq:subscriber doc:name="Subscriber" config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config" destination="myQueue">
  <anypoint-mq:subscriber-type >
    <anypoint-mq:polling >
      <scheduling-strategy >
        <fixed-frequency />
      </scheduling-strategy>
    </anypoint-mq:polling>
  </anypoint-mq:subscriber-type>
</anypoint-mq:subscriber>
```

The default `polling` subscriber-type declaration attempts to retrieve 10 messages from the declared
destination every 1 second, dispatching the retrieved messages to the flow individually as a
`MuleMessage` instance.

The resulting `MuleMessage` has:

- The message body as its payload
- The message metadata in the message attributes

![Mule message output, including payload and message attributes](_images/amq-4x-subscriber-output.png)

You can customize the Subscriber source polling strategy using the standard Mule runtime engine
scheduling strategies:

- [Fixed Frequency](../../mule-runtime/latest/scheduler-concept#fixed-frequency)
- [Cron Expressions](../../mule-runtime/latest/scheduler-concept#cron-expressions)

### Customized Fixed Polling

You can customize the frequency at which the Subscriber source polls for new messages by declaring a
`polling` subscriber-type strategy with a customized `fixed-frequency` scheduling strategy.

The default `fixed-frequency` scheduling strategy declaration looks like this:

![Subscriber configuration window showing Scheduling Strategy set to Fixed Frequency](_images/amq-4x-subscriber-polling-fixed.png)

```xml
<anypoint-mq:subscriber config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config" destination="myQueue">
  <anypoint-mq:subscriber-type >
    <anypoint-mq:polling>
      <scheduling-strategy >
        <fixed-frequency/>
      </scheduling-strategy>
    </anypoint-mq:polling>
  </anypoint-mq:subscriber-type>
</anypoint-mq:subscriber>
```

### Cron-Based Polling

The Anypoint MQ Subscriber can also use a `cron` scheduling strategy, which enables you to schedule
jobs such as "Run every minute starting at 2pm and ending at 2:59pm, every day". To use this
scheduling strategy, declare a `polling` subscriber-type strategy with a customized `cron`
scheduling strategy:

![Subscriber configuration window showing Scheduling Strategy set to Cron](_images/amq-4x-subscriber-polling-cron.png)

```xml
<anypoint-mq:subscriber config-ref="Anypoint_MQ_Config" destination="myQueue">
  <anypoint-mq:subscriber-type >
    <anypoint-mq:polling >
      <scheduling-strategy >
        <cron expression="0 * 14 * * ?" timeZone="America/Los_Angeles" />
      </scheduling-strategy>
    </anypoint-mq:polling>
  </anypoint-mq:subscriber-type>
</anypoint-mq:subscriber>
```

## Message Acknowledgment

You can acknowledge received messages automatically only if the flow execution finishes successfully
or as your app consumes them, before processing them.

- **Automatic**

  By default, the Subscriber source uses the `AUTO` acknowledgment mode. With this mode, the
  messages that the Subscriber source retrieves are acknowledged automatically after message flow
  processing succeeds. This means that the Subscriber source receives a message, dispatches it to
  the flow, and waits to see how the message processing finishes. It executes an ACK only when the
  processing finishes without exceptions.

  If the execution of the processing flow finishes with a propagated exception, the message is
  automatically not acknowledged and is returned to the queue for redelivery.

  For more information, see [Automatic Acknowledgment](anypoint-mq-ack#automatic-acknowledgment).

- **Immediate**

  When you use the `IMMEDIATE` acknowledgment mode, the consumed message is acknowledged (deleted
  from the queue) and then dispatched to the Mule flow for processing. If the message acknowledgment
  fails, the message is discarded. The message isnât dispatched to the flow and remains in-flight
  until the acknowledgment timeout.

  If the app restarts between being acknowledged and dispatched, the message might not be available
  again because it was deleted from the queue. To prevent message deletion until after processing,
  use the `AUTO` or `MANUAL` acknowledgment mode instead.

  Using the Subscriber source in Prefetch mode with the `IMMEDIATE` acknowledgment mode sometimes
  results in thread accumulation and an unresponsive application.

  For more information, see [Immediate Acknowledgment](anypoint-mq-ack#immediate-acknowledgment).

- **Manual**

  When you use the `MANUAL` acknowledgment mode, the app logic decides when to perform the
  acknowledgment of the message, using the [ACK](anypoint-mq-ack) or [NACK](anypoint-mq-ack)
  sources.

  To perform the manual acknowledgment, you need the value of `ackToken` provided as part of the
  resulting message attributes.

  For more information, see [Manual Acknowledgment](anypoint-mq-ack#manual-acknowledgment).

For information about acknowledgment timeouts, see
[Acknowledgment Timeout](anypoint-mq-ack#acknowledgment-timeout).

## Circuit Breaker Capability

The Subscriber source provides circuit breaking capability, which enables you to control how the
connector handles errors that occur while processing a consumed message.

For example, when connecting to an external service, you can use the circuit breaker to handle any
downtime of that service. The circuit breaker allows the system to stop making requests and allows
the external service to recover under a reduced load.

Using Anypoint MQ in a Mule 4 app means having a Mule flow with an MQ subscriber that consumes
messages from a queue and processes them using an external service. When this service isnât
available:

1.  The request fails.
2.  An error results.
3.  Message processing finishes as either a failure or as a custom error for handling the message,
    such as sending it to a dead letter queue (DLQ).

When the external service is not available, every attempt to process a message results in a failure,
forcing the app to loop, consuming messages that cannot succeed. You can avoid this behavior by
notifying the subscriber of the error in a way that prevents it from consuming more messages for a
certain period.

### Circuit Breaker Processes

The circuit breaker capability that the Subscriber source provides is bound to the error handling
mechanism provided by Mule. It uses the error notification mechanism to count errors related to an
external service, which is known as a circuit failure. You can bind any error to a circuit failure.
For example, you can bind `HTTP:TIMEOUT`, `FTP:SERVICE_NOT_AVAILABLE`, or even a custom error from
your app, such as `ORG:EXTERNAL_ERROR`.

If a Mule flow finishes its execution with an error, the Subscriber source checks if the error is
one of the `onErrorTypes` that indicates an external service error, and counts consecutive
occurrences until `errorsThreshold` is reached.

When `errorsThreshold` is reached, the circuit trips and remains open for the duration specified by
`tripTimeout`. While the circuit is open, all polled or consumed messages are not acknowledged
(NACK) and Anypoint MQ halts any future operations (poll, ACK, NACK, and Publish) until
`tripTimeout` elapses.

After `tripTimeout` elapses, the circuit breaker transitions to a new
[state](#circuit-breaker-states). If the new state is **Closed**, clients can consume messages on
the next poll.

The behavior varies depending on whether you deploy the app to CloudHub with a single worker or
multiple workers:

Single worker

The worker processes messages individually from the queue and opens the circuit breaker when the
number of errors reaches the value of `errorsThreshold`.

Multiple workers

Each worker has an associated circuit breaker and tracks the values of `errorsThreshold` and
`tripTimeout` individually. Each worker processes messages individually from the queue and opens the
circuit breaker when the number of errors reaches the value of `errorsThreshold` set on the worker.

By default, the circuit breaking feature is disabled.

### Circuit Breaker States

The circuit breaker has three states: Closed, Open, and Half Open. The behavior of the app changes
based on the current state. See the
[Microsoft Circuit Breaker pattern](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/patterns/circuit-breaker)
for more information.

![Flow showing the states of the circuit breaker: closed, half-open, and open](_images/amq-4x-subscriber-states-view.png)

- **Closed**

  The starting state where the Subscriber source retrieves messages normally from MQ based on its
  configuration, effectively working as if the circuit breaker is not present.

- **Closed-Open Transition**

  When the number of failures occurs in succession during message processing, without successes, and
  reaches the `errorsThreshold` value, the circuit breaker trips and the circuit breaker transitions
  to an Open state.

  Messages that were already dispatched to the flow then finish processing, regardless of whether
  the result is success or failure.

  Messages kept locally that are in-flight for the broker but havenât been dispatched yet are not
  acknowledged and returned to the queue for redelivery to another consumer.

- **Open**

  The Subscriber source doesnât attempt to retrieve messages, and skips the iterations silently
  until `tripTimeout` is reached.

- **Half Open**

  After `tripTimeout` elapses, the Subscriber source goes to a Half Open state. In the next poll for
  messages, the Subscriber source retrieves a single message from the service and uses that message
  to check if the system has recovered before going back to the normal Closed state.

  When the Subscriber source successfully fetches a single message, dispatches it to the flow, and
  processing finishes successfully, the Subscriber source returns to normal and immediately attempts
  to fetch more messages.

  If Mule flow processing fails with one of the expected `onErrorTypes`, the circuit goes back to an
  Open state and resets the `tripTimeout` timer.

### Configure the Circuit Breaker

You can configure a Circuit Breaker as either a Global Circuit Breaker or a Private Circuit Breaker.

Either way, the configuration parameters are the same:

- `onErrorTypes`

  The error types that count as a failure during the flow execution. An error occurrence counts only
  when the flow finishes with an error propagation. By default, all errors count as a circuit
  failure.

- `errorsThreshold`

  The number of `onErrorTypes` errors that must occur for the circuit breaker to open.

- `tripTimeout`

  How long the circuit remains open once `errorsThreshold` is reached.

- `circuitName`

  The name of a circuit breaker to bind to this configuration. By default, each queue has its own
  circuit breaker.

#### Global Circuit Breaker

Use a Global Circuit Breaker when you want to share the circuit state across multiple subscribers,
as if subscribers are part of the same "circuit".

1.  In Anypoint Studio, click the **Global Elements** tab in the canvas.
2.  Select **Create > Component Configuration > Circuit Breaker**.

    ![Circuit breaker configuration window including error types, error threshold, trip timeout, and trip timeout unit](_images/amq-4x-cb-global-canvas.png)

    In the configuration wizard, populate the following fields as needed. Once the configuration is
    complete, you can reference this `Circuit_breaker` declaration from any Anypoint MQ Subscriber.

To reference a circuit breaker:

1.  Select the Subscriber source in the canvas.
2.  Click the **Advanced** tab.
3.  Select **Circuit Breaker > Global Reference** and select a global circuit breaker configuration
    from the list.

    ![Subscriber configuration window showing Global reference set to Circuit_breaker](_images/amq-4x-cb-global-ref.png)

#### Private Circuit Breaker

You declare a Private Circuit Breaker internally on a single subscriber. This circuit declaration is
used only in the flow where the Subscriber source is declared, isolated from all the other circuits.

To use this configuration:

1.  Select the Subscriber source in the canvas.
2.  Click the **Advanced** tab.
3.  Select **Circuit breaker > Edit Inline**, and then complete the fields.

    ![Subscriber configuration window showing Circuit breaker set to Edit Inline](_images/amq-4x-cb-inline.png)

### Circuit Breaker Examples

#### Circuit Configuration for a Single Subscriber

In this example, a single subscriber consumes messages from a queue and posts the messages to
another service using its REST API. You can stop processing messages after 5 requests to the
